title: Tainted Grail: The Fall of Avalon
Review Text: Act 3. I'm not going to go into detail or post spoilers, but Act 3 feels like an entirely different dev team made it. NPCs vanish, quest markers bug out and also vanish, key NPCs lack voiceovers, every quest is fetch and return, and the environment is incredibly annoying to traverse. From both my experience and others I have spoken to who have played this game, you are LESS likely to completely finish the multi-part quests (not gonna spoil it) than you are to experience a quest-breaking bug and have to skip it entirely. Or, if you are me, do 8 hours of the quest for it to break in the final part. Speaking of quests! Quest Rewards in the latter part of Act 2 and all of Act 3 are bad to say the least. Sometimes you wont even get a reward, sometimes you'll get a rock and some arrows, and other times you get a cool endgame item. Obviously the former two are much more common, which is why this falls under "the bad". Another segue, arrows! Arrows are all that is sold by blacksmiths in Act 3. Seriously, that is it. No weapons, often times no crafting components, just arrows. It's made all the more annoying by the fact that where are 5+ merchants in Act 3, and they ALL have barren stock. Next up is the story, and this one is my second biggest irritant. Up until the end of Act 2, the story is fantastic, fun, mysterious, and dark. Once you enter Act 3, there is no longer a story. That's right. It's gone. Instead, the devs give you the fetch quest to end all fetch quests, ending with a final boss fight. Finally, the thing I hated the absolute most in the game: The enemies of the final dungeon in the 3rd area. I won't state what they are, only that they are a spongey and slow slog to fight. They aren't particularly deadly at this point in the game, just ridiculously tanky and INSANELY loud. Like, gives a loud verbal feedback on EVERY HIT against them loud. Fights with these enemies drown out the music as you sit there for 5 minutes wailing on them, and in return they deafeningly yell over, and over, and over, and over.

Review Text: Wow this game really surprised me. I usually wait a year after a game has been released before I buy it and I would never buy one in EA but I tried the demo and was pleasantly surprised and bought it. I really love and I'm grateful for that it has different difficulty levels I can play on, I have brain damage and spent a good 40 hours on the easiest level learning how to play it then I made another character and was able to play it on a higher level and fished the main quest but not all the sides so now I'll do another one and try again even harder. There is a lot to explore so climb,swim,cook,brew potions and meet some interesting NPCs and just have fun. I look forward to when more content is ready to be released. I waited to play this again until the full release and all I can say is WOOHOO!!! ,you got this old gamer doing the happy dance while spinning in circles in my gaming chair and singing get down with the wyrdness !!! Thanks again for a wonderful game it has given me alot of joy.

title: Tainted Grail: The Fall of Avalon
Review Text: I liked the game, but the further I go the more clear it is that this game should not have left early access. It's buggy, voice lines are frequently missing or broken, including in the main quest, and the dungeons include such exciting locales as "Scene/Ending_Alchemist_Dungeon". I have no idea if the branching narrative the description advertises is there, but I have seen several main quests that you can do out of order by exploring, and your only reward is confusing or frustrating dialogue that either references various things you never saw or did or refuses to acknowledge the things you did out of order. By act 3 it feels like the game really loses steam and the side quests become far less interesting while the bugs get worse and worse. It also seems like the devs simply decided to not animate several non-human npcs in act 3, so when you speak to them they just stand there and stare at you with their mouths closed while audio plays. The world is interesting and the game is fun, to a point, but by the end of the game you're just steamrolling enemies and rushing through buggy quests trying to get to the end.

Review Text: I could at least understand if they were trying to pivot RE4 into a proper survival horror game, you can kinda them attempting it in some areas. But this is still a rollercoaster of setpieces with inconsequential puzzles. You’re going to be killing everything down the one path instead of counting bullets or trying to determine the best route to backtrack across the mansion with. So this remake is the worst of both worlds where it's still structured like a corridor shooter but the great combat and fun banter has been gutted and replaced with generic, contemporary ideas. This game is a skinwalker.

title: Train Sim World® 6
Review Text: Excellent game/program.  I have worked on the railroad for over 59 years.  Mostly steam, and now diesel electric.  Of course, you are not going to get the full feeling of being on a locomotive with a computer program, but I can honestly say that this is my favorite program, and I don't play trains on computers, unless instructing for the railroad.  Again, not computer program can perfect the actual activity or experience, but this Train World 5 and 6 do a great job.  I was running the Jubilee last night and it kept feeling like I was running the 750, 290, or other steam locomotives, except for the left hand engineers side, but even the lubricator hand crank works.  I hear other people complaining about this or that improper fitting, etc., but, as one of my mentors, initials B.P. use to say, "I don't care if it is painted purple with white pooka dots, as long as its running."  Yes, its running, and running well.  Brings back the feelings of running down the mainline at night and seeing people run out of churches and houses waving towels and waving, as the coal fired engines would tear through the night.  And today, you can see myself and two others running the 290 in Fried Green Tomatoes, with my 5 chime central of Ga. Whistle on it.  Buddy was not hurt in the making of that film.  Lol  So, great job, to the builders of this program.  I highly recommend it.  And, I love the Boston stuff, as my steam beginnings were at Edaville RR and Steamtown, in Vermont.  Thanks again and carry on!  Over and Out.  And, no one paid me for this endorsement.  I bought it on this fantastic sale, which ends on January 5th. If you like trains, they will run.  Have fun.  Clear Signa!

Review Text: Some things I would like to see improved/added is the horrible texture/shadow pop in. Depending on your zoom level the scene looks drastically different and can be quite annoying. I always feel like there are redundant screens to get to the same or slightly different information. The overall UI for Creators Club could use some work too, not enough filters and no information on custom made scenarios besides what the author chooses to include in the description. An FOV slider would be nice. The washed out lighting and lens flare seems to have gotten worse, on certain routes some signals can be hard to determine their color as well. Its annoying to keep having to recommend features from a game that has been out for so long now but we desperately need labeled platforms, portals, and sidings and trains like TSC has. This goes a long way when learning a route and trying to build scenarios. I just don't get why simple features from the previous series were not brought over.

title: Warhammer 40,000: Space Marine 2
Review Text: Requiring an online connection for all unlocks and progression is plain BS.  If you have even the tiniest interruption in internet for whatever reason, and the game disconnects you and shoves you into offline mode.  None of your progress in offline mode counts for any unlocks and gets erased once you go back to the title screen to reconnect or restart the game.  That's right, there is no auto-reconnect or option to retry connection, so you can't preserve any of your progress if you happen to get disconnected for even just a split second.  I have lost over 20 hours of progress thanks to this.  I don't usually post reviews, but this was so frustrating that it warranted one.

title: Warhammer 40,000: Space Marine 2
Review Text: i wish steam had an in-between option for reviews, because while the gameplay is fun, i don't think it's worth the price with the current level of content. there's a fairly short (6 mission) campaign, 6 operations missions which play out alongside the main campaign, and a barebones PVP mode which is okay. The combat is good but can be a bit janky, especially the melee combat. the control scheme isn't great for mouse & keyboard and was obviously designed with console controllers in mind. I had a few weird bugs, including character/weapon/enemy models becoming invisible & connection issues trying to play operations with a friend, but i appreciate it's still very early days and these will likely be fixed. i'll almost definitely come back to this game in future if/when they add more content, but currently it's far too expensive for what you get.

title: Warhammer 40,000: Space Marine 2
Review Text: The single player campaign is pretty good, but this game's servers in multiplayer Operations mode are some of the worst I have ever seen in a game, and I'm not exaggerating. From being kicked off of the server mid game (or even at the tail end of a mission), to having to go back to the title screen to rejoin multiplayer every time the system inexplicably decides to take you offline, this is by far the worst multiplayer experience I have ever had in a game. How this game's multiplayer servers are still in such a sorry state after nearly a year is beyond me. I've just deleted this game from my PC out of frustration, and I won't be coming back until the devs fix these server issues. I don't care how many skins or updates they release if the underlying servers continue to be so shamefully bad.

I keep seeing people suggesting a clean reinstall and I haven't tested to see if that would work, purely for the fact that my internet connection is shared and I'm not hardwired. The act of doing so would take hours that I do not care to spend. Especially as it means that that's time that I essentially can do nothing involving the internet whatsoever. If I uninstall this game, it will likely be a permanent choice. I cannot put my faith in continuing to be able to play a game I spent money for if a single update breaks it for so many users AND then the development team has the absolute GALL to imply that those of us having issues don't know how to do something as minor as update our own graphics cards. title: Monster Hunter Wilds
Review Text: Wilds is an autopilot version of Monster Hunter I used to love. Optimizing gear no longer matters; the monsters die no matter what. There's no need to learn the maps; your mount automatically takes you to the monsters, which are now permanently visible on the map. Monsters just give two or three times the rewards that they used to, so no need to hunt a monster more than a few times for the weapon you want. In trying to innovate, Capcom broke the feedback loop I knew and love, and I hope that before they release the expansion, they remember that Monster Hunter was great BECAUSE of friction, not in spite of it.

title: ARK: Survival Ascended
Review Text: I waited to write a review.  I wanted to see how things go.  Every mod that is really interesting goes premium.  Which is fine, but things I would have normally tried on ASE, I am skipping, including maps.  The thing is, the base game plus DLC is the price of a premium game.  And now there are more DLC, pay to play dinos.  It is just too much too quickly.  ASE was about no microtransactions.  I really support the idea of mods getting rewarded by placing their mods under premium, I just cannot spend more and more money, so its only a bummer for me.  This is just ark.  There is nothing new unless you buy DLC or mods.  Same bugs, same issues, same stories and gameplay.  But this time, you are forced to have there DLC in game, even if you don't have it (you just cannot tame creatures but they sure can kill you).  My desire to continue is just gone, its not fun anymore unless I pay for something.  Even the extra dinos added to each map launch are lackluster due to all this "buy this fantastic tame, buy this DLC, buy this mod" ... and those new creatures we voted for and were excited just kind of get forgotten about.   I wouldn't recommend it anymore to anyone because it is now a money pit and the premise of the original game is overshadowed by that.  It is a shame, I have dumped hours and hours into it but at the end of the day, I would rather just find a different game to play.

title: ARK: Survival Ascended
Review Text: I love this game with everything in me. Honest to god, its one of my favorite games of all time. I have ADHD, and so it can be hard for me to stay interested in a game for a long period of time, but not this one. I can easily sink hundreds and hundreds of hours into this game and it'll always be so much fun. However, there's a serious issue that needs to be addressed. Greed, and Laziness. The developers are fairly lazy. There's bugs in the game that have existed for many many years now. The same bugs in the old game, on the new one. If they built it from the ground up again, how could that be? It seems to me like they got lazy, and started copy and pasting code or something. If you're not sure the remake is worth 40$, rest assured, it isn't. Unless you have a really powerful computer, you're gonna have to end up playing on low settings anyways. A remaster with the same bugs and issues, years and years later. How can you call that a remaster? Disgraceful. We also have to talk about the greed. While i still feel wildcard is greedy, i don't think they're actually that bad. The real problem is their parent company snail games, as well as their server provider nitrado. Snail games is incredibly greedy. They please their shareholders by making pump and dump survival games, that they always eventually abandon. Ark is their golden goose. Its the one they put the most care into upholding. Yet they still ♥♥♥♥ in everyone's cereal, constantly. Don't forget that this remaster was going to be free. There's even been fiasco's in the past where snail games employees get raided, and they just ban the people who raided them off of the official server network. However the greediest of them all, is 100% without a doubt nitrado. Basically to sum it all up, wildcard was in debt and needed servers. Nitrado offered to loan them servers, but they wanted to exclusive rights for server hosting on Ark Survival Ascended. Wildcard obliged, and so now nitrado holds a complete monopoly over server hosting. The CHEAPEST server you can buy is 25$ a month for 20 slots. If you know anything about renting servers, you'll know thats pretty abhorrent. Thats incredibly expensive for servers that run like absolute ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ anyways. Constant Rubber-Banding, teleporting all of the place, rollbacks, insane amounts of lag so much so offical network is literally unplayable for me right now. I'm supposed to live about 150 miles away from where the server is hosted, yet i always get 150 ping on NA servers. Its atrocious. I know snail games will never change their greedy ways, but i can at least hope the server hosting situation gets sorted because at this point i can't afford to host a server for just me and a few friends. And even if i could, it would suck anyways. If you wanna buy it, i totally get the appeal. But do not support this kind of behavior. Wait till its on sale, or the issues have been fixed. I love Ark, it just deserves better management.

I understand that it was marketed with working crossplay and a lot of people were looking forward to this feature in particular but i do not believe that this game deserves a 65% rating just because the crossplay is broken and because people didnt expect the survival horror game to use disempowerment even though this is the most common way to make people afraid in the genre. It is a very interesting and really good game. Please give it a try. The atmosphere is so thick you could cut it with a knife and the team really outdid themselves on the sanity mechanics!

title: The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu
Review Text: EDIT: Do not buy as a solo player. There is little enjoyment to be had. You get an AI companion that is simultaneously completely useless and absolutely cracked. He has an unbreakable machete, a crossbow with infinite ammo, and he can revive you without ammonia salts that you would typically need. The same is inversely true where you can revive him for no cost. His AI is abysmal, he just goes off on side quests and you won't see him for long periods of time. This means that you are losing sanity much faster as you are alone. Sometimes he won't revive you because he doesn't feel like it. He will not carry things like deer back to the wagon, but he has a special obsession with firewood, which he will seek out and bring to the cart for some reason. He prioritizes firewood over anything else, including you. His AI has no regard for if you are between him and an enemy. He killed me on several occasions playing solo. You can also recover his crossbow bolts, so if you have a crossbow then you both now have infinite ammo.

title: The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu

title: The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu
Review Text: Otherwise its great. The atmosphere is creepy and the game looks amazing. I do think this shouldve been released in early access though and then get improved over time. Hell I even think they could further expand this with new levels, mechanics, items and many more elements.

title: The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu
Review Text: Having weapons need to be recovered once before they are unlocked for selection, rather than just a random selection of items would be nice. Right now it feels like there just isn't a reason to continue to do missions, other than to watch randos who don't talk die horribly after they run off from the main group. There will be people that flame me for saying that, but it feels a bit too much like the game mechanics are being pulled in many different directions to the satisfaction of nobody. It's not a hide-and-survive horror game (you can't hide, your only option is to detour widely and hope), its not a fast extraction-type game(because the game punishes you hard for movement noise), its not a hack-and-slash-raider like Darktide(because the combat systems are limited and tilted against the player). Its a semi-functional thing in-between these for me, and it just doesn't quite work.

title: Hades II
Review Text: The ending of this game completely ruined my entire experience. The narrative feels completely pointless, like they had built up this dramatic story and had absolutely no idea how to finish it in a way that made sense or one that was fulfilling. It left me with absolutely no desire to continue the post game. There are several end game momments that just feel like it's making fun of the player for being interested or invested in the narrative, like it was silly of me to care, the conclusion of the hypnos arc is a great example and really felt like a slap in the face.   If you are interested in story do not play this, it will feel like a huge waste of time. HADES 1 is a much better experience in every regard and HADES 2 throws a wet blanket on the entire world narrative. If I could erase this game from my memory I would.

title: Hades II
Review Text: If it wasn't for my leftover love for the first game, I would not have made it THIS far. I would have dropped it. One too many "eh" feeling runs where the final result was a handful of meta resources, none of which added enough to actually let me do anything new. I just kept plugging away hoping that I'd finally unlock some last new feature that would ramp it all back up, like new defensive mechanics, or better weapon aspects (kinda, but not enough to overcome build woes) and 20 hours in it has not come. I'm not sure just how far along the game is within this early access. I hope it's not far, but its rough enough that I actually think it was a mistake to make it available this early on. Not a strong enough first impression coming off the heels of such a lauded first title.

title: Hades II

title: Hades II
Review Text: The story was pretty coherent throughout, the characters are fun and interesting until the game suddenly decides that not only does Melinoe not have any agency anymore, nobody does! To make it even better, none of the characters in the game itself understand how the ending works. If you write an ending where every single character involved is as unhappy and confused as the player, is it even a good ending? It's bad to the point where I would love to know if whoever wrote it was normally that misogynistic, or if they just stumbled into it while writing the last few hours of the main story. I don't even want to finish the epilogue for completion's sake.

title: Hades II

I honestly believe we shouldn't tell the developers that these mechanics are okay and praise them for it, but 94% positive reviews are going to tell them a different story. Which is sad. I'm probably going to finish this game once it comes out of Early Access, but that doesn't mean I'm going to like these new mechanics they put in the game to artifically increase the amount of time you have to spend grinding instead of enjoying the game in order to make progress and their balance decisions. It's a real shame that Hades is a lot better than Hades II, I've been waiting for this game and was really hyped, but the experience I got was simply disappointing.

Review Text: I rarely leave reviews, but here we go. Hades has been one of my favorite pieces of media for years, yet when I finally reached the first ending, I felt nothing—just empty. Honestly, I’m going to treat it like the Boruto sequel and pretend it doesn’t exist. Play it if you just want more content, but don’t expect much from the story. Just saying again: it’s not a bad game. But the ending was so disappointing—it had so much potential. I can say this after playing 150 hours, and it’s still one of my favorites. If you loved the characters from the first game, this is probably NOT the sequel you’ve been waiting for.

title: Warhammer 40,000: Darktide
Review Text: I am on the same page as many others. As an early adopter of this game on Steam, we Beta tested the game for them, and then essentially suffered through beta-phases of every mechanic in the game as they ironed things out before the console releases over a year later. FatShark has now spit in our faces and compensated us nothing for the play-testing while giving away more than 10x the amount of MTX currency for new adopters of the game. They offer us a paid upgrade for 1/10th of the currency they're giving new, console players for simply purchasing the game. I absolutely love this game, but I feel FatShark has treated the Steam players that have supported them from the beginning poorly while trying to appeal to the players who could be a fresh revenue stream for them.

title: Warhammer 40,000: Darktide
Review Text: Each weapon type, be it axes or lasguns or shotguns, has different variants with alternate fire modes, bonuses and so on, but at the end of the day you’ll go for the best accuracy and damage, because spamming a thousand bullets is meaningless if you don’t hit. With one melee and one ranged weapon each, characters have options when it comes to their role in the field, and a coordinated team may choose who takes care of the rabble and who focuses on specials and bosses. title: Warhammer 40,000: Darktide
Review Text: - I could keep writing reasons why you shouldn't buy this game, but I will not as many have done so before me already. The core gameplay loop is amazing, despite its flaws, and here in a year or so i'm sure it will polish out. But you shouldn't buy this game. Fat Shark already made two tide games, the second entry in the series being a prime example of a phenomenal change from bad to good as the team polished the game up significantly over the years. But all of that progress is gone, all of the strides they'd made with QOL and gameplay, balance, combat, communication, transparency, etc have all been washed away. Somehow being the third Tide title, they've taken 2 steps backwards and none forwards. The entire community is very confused as to how the game has turned out as it has, and the fumbling of the Community Managers with regard to responding to these questions and criticisms has done nothing but evaporate goodwill and seed doubt. Despite what Hedge (a prominent community manager) will tell you, this game is in Early Access, and if you've played it you know it, regardless of how fun and enjoyable it may be. The game is not finished, and even with it being as fun as it is, it's not worth 40$ by any stretch of the imagination. Buy it if you like fun early access games or being a beta tester, but if you want a polished product you'll be left wanting. I suggest checking back in a year.

title: Resident Evil Requiem
Review Text: Capcom really created a masterpiece—and I’m not talking about the game engine.

title: Resident Evil Requiem
Review Text: What stood out the most to me is how well the game manages to capture the spirit of the older survival horror entries while still feeling modern in the way it plays. It really feels like the developers tried to bring back that balance between exploration, tension, and resource management that made the earlier titles so memorable, the story is told in a gradual way and doesn’t immediately explain everything to the player, which honestly makes it much more engaging. Very early on, the game sets up a mystery around the events that happened before you arrive, as you progress, you slowly uncover pieces of information through the environments, documents you find, and certain interactions with characters. It encourages the player to pay attention and connect the dots themselves rather than just giving all the answers right away, without going into spoilers, the narrative stays very faithful to the classic themes that have always defined the series. You still get that sense of dangerous experiments, situations spiraling out of control, and the consequences that come with people trying to push science too far, there are also several references to elements longtime fans will recognize, but they are integrated naturally and don’t feel forced. If you’ve played previous entries in the franchise, there are a few moments that quietly acknowledge the past without relying entirely on nostalgia, the characters are also quite well handled, at first, it’s not always clear what everyone’s motivations are, which adds a layer of uncertainty to the story, as the game progresses, you begin to understand them better, and that slow reveal keeps you curious about what will happen next, atmosphere is easily one of the strongest aspects of the game, the environments are extremely detailed and often feel oppressive in the best way possible, many of the locations feel like places with a real history behind them, and small environmental details hint at what might have happened before the player arrived. It really adds to the immersion and makes exploration rewarding, the game also does a great job at building tension, there are plenty of moments where you move slowly through an area simply because you’re not sure what might be waiting around the next corner. Sometimes even hearing a distant sound in another room is enough to make you stop and think before moving forward, that kind of atmosphere strongly reminds me of the older entries that focused more on suspense and dread rather than constant action, gameplay wise, it sticks closely to the survival horror formula, resources are limited, so every bullet matters, you often have to decide whether it’s worth fighting an enemy or if it’s smarter to avoid them and save your ammo. Inventory management also plays an important role since you can’t carry everything you find, that forces you to plan ahead and think carefully about what you bring with you, puzzles are present throughout the game as well, and they’re implemented nicely, they’re not overly complicated, but they still require observation and a bit of thought, some of them also encourage you to revisit areas after finding certain items or clues elsewhere, which makes exploration feel more interconnected, from a technical perspective, the game looks great, the environments are highly detailed, and the lighting especially stands out in darker areas where shadows and limited visibility play a big role in the atmosphere, the creature designs are also very well done and sometimes genuinely unsettling, sound design deserves a lot of credit too, footsteps, distant noises, creaking doors, and subtle background sounds all contribute to the constant feeling of tension, of course, it’s not completely perfect, there are a few moments that lean a bit more toward action compared to the rest of the experience, which can slightly break the slower survival horror pacing if you prefer the more suspenseful sections, enemy AI can also behave a little strangely on rare occasions, but it’s nothing that seriously affects the overall experience, even with those minor flaws, the overall experience is extremely solid, the atmosphere is excellent, exploration feels rewarding, and the story keeps you interested without revealing everything too quickly, you can clearly feel the influence of the older titles while still benefiting from modern gameplay and presentation, for longtime fans of the series, there’s a lot here to appreciate, and even for someone who is newer to this type of survival horror experience, it’s still a very strong and memorable game :)

title: Resident Evil Requiem

The more I think about the story, the agrier I get how it just doesn't make any sense, it's just convenience after convenience. Resident Evil 9 Retcoiem is a trainwreck, a pretty and fun trainwreck, you cannot stop looking at it. It makes the already convoluted timeline of RE even more jumbled, worse, contradictory.

title: Resident Evil Requiem
Review Text: The game is honestly overhyped. The constant switching between characters kills any sense of rhythm or immersion, making it hard to enjoy the flow of the game. The police station callback felt empty, and it’s weird that Leon doesn’t even acknowledge Claire or Ada it just breaks continuity. On top of that, the game is so overpriced for what you get, especially with no DLCs or Mercenaries mode. Leon’s section feels more like Devil May Cry than Resident Evil, which just doesn’t fit. And seriously… who even is Zeno?

title: Resident Evil Requiem
Review Text: Overall, I think they tried to combine all the RE games' mechanics and came out with something disappointing trying to please everyone. Instead of Grace and Leon having the same unhurried pace in all of their movements, I wish they were separate so we can enjoy two dedicated play styles.

title: Resident Evil Requiem

title: Derail Valley
Review Text: Masterpiece of a game and excellent execution of a train simulator, Dovetail games  sims struggle to keep my interest more than a few hours, but this game ive logged almost as much time into as ive done with war thunder and GTA V.  The devs care about this game and still support it with the same attention to detail as they did when overhauled came out. Each update brought noticeable changes, from improvements to tracks to allow higher speeds in 2020, to new industries and even a museum in the update that was released just minutes before the posting of this review. If you have a minor interest in trains and railways, i reccommend this game, it wont let you down.

title: Cat Mail Co.
Review Text: Once your shift is over, the boat captain will show up and you unload the mail he delivers for the cats in your town and sort that, and then he'll tell you where he's delivering the mail next. That's when you take the packages that match the destination with where he's going next and load them up onto the boat. This is where things get interesting. Each time he shows up, different items you can't move will be on his boat in different designs each time, and so you have to fit the boxes in a sort of puzzle to maximize the amount of packages you can ship out. I find that really fun and enjoy seeing just how much I can fit on the ship with the different layouts I'm forced to load up. I recommend this game to anyone who likes cozy, repetitive games like this. I'm still not bored yet!

title: Cat Mail Co.
Review Text: My biggest want is to be able to place boxes across MULTIPLE different boxes. Being confined to only being able to set the same(or smaller) box types on top of boxes makes 0 sense. If I have a 2x2 and a 2x3 next to each other, as long as the top half is flat, I should be able to put a box across *both* of those boxes.

title: Cat Mail Co.
Review Text: Hiwever, I wish there was a bit more dynamic play with the boat. I would love to have options to make it a bit harder and more interesting, like certain packages can and cannot be sent at different times to make the day/night boats have more purpose (cold and dark packages can only go at night, hot and light packages can only go at day). A "weather event" system where you can't send fragile packages because it's storming would also be cool. I love a good challenge and wish there was a way to track that in the game.

title: Cat Mail Co.
Review Text: I will give you the positive review for the cozy game. HOWEVER, it is conditional. The game is fun and initially organizing and compartmentalizing things is great. This gradually becomes an issue however as someone mentioned in another review people just don't pick ♥♥♥♥ up. There ends up not being enough space to organize things the way I want. Once you get the restrictions (I.e. cold room, etc) there aren't enough shelves for destinations nor enough places for large boxes. Toward the end of the game EVERY ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ package had a mate and I was making designs out of stamps to keep it all organized. So the first couple hours were great, really scratched that OCD organizing itch, the last couple hours I craved death and wanted to throw every package that got dropped off for deliver into the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ water. There simply wasnt enough room on the boat and we had WAY to many large packages. Add a decline button for drop offs, or restrict night to pickup only, they can take those 4 package drop offs and shove em. Anyyyway, TLDR: cute game for the first half, wanted to kill myself trying to get the completion for the later half.

title: Cat Mail Co.
Review Text: Right now, the game is effectively the demo gameplay loop, albeit longer and with a small handful of additional mechanics. I was really hoping for some 'oh, that's cool' moments in the full game - a way to upgrade the boat, employ additional boats to amplify or add different destinations, reorganise shelves so they make more sense for the packages, deal with customers that want to send and collect, some bit of story etc, but as others have said this feels barebones and gets pretty tedious quickly. For the price, I don't feel cheated and have had a few hours of fun (the core mechanics and art style are solid) but I was sad to find that's where it stops. Putting this down for now and will see if / how the game develops in time.

It's also annoying to have multiple packages dropped off but each cat only seems to pick up one at a time. Some of the dialogue describing the packages are vague, like there are multiple packages that look like or have the stickers or name that the cat is asking for so its frustrating to bring the cat multiple packages that are wrong. title: Borderlands 4
Review Text: Once they fix their game engine, this will likely be regarded as a gem.  That's for the future to prove me right or wrong.  But until then, BL4 will remain divisive and representative of the class war between the principles of AAA game design and the desires of the "non-premium" gamer.  Pitchford is a douche thumbing his nose at those who can't afford a 1500 dollar gfx card.  Fire him and find someone who will let the community guide the future of this game and BL4 will be a redemption story.  It's such a solid foundation sitting in a swamp of technical limitations it didn't even need to have (five year old games look better and run at 120fps base).  Give us all a couple of paid DLCs for free and focus on optimization for 3-6 months and the community will likely turn friendly.  It's unfair to those who made this game so good that BL4 is the battleground between AAA shareholder stroking and mainstream gamers' hopes and dreams.

I’d use Maliwan Takedown as inspiration—there should be 10 to 20 unique, linear levels like that. Review Text: I installed this just to leave a review to tell IOI to go !@#$ themselves for this game's egregious monetization.  !@#$ you for making me think I was actually buying most of the game when I guess I just got part ($30 for an inferior version of the game and they want $70 MORE for the full game), !@#$ you for not making it EVEN CLEARER and easy for people to buy/gift and play your game, and more importantly !@#$ you for splitting this into 4 versions plus 13 DLC (half are hidden/buried) plus Deluxe Pack purchases, and most importantly !@#$ you for not listening to your community and making this scummy !@#$ right.  Just look at the !@#$ing reviews for nearly every piece of content associated with this game.  What the actual !@#$ is with this Part One bull!@#$?  Just have three versions of the game and make ALL DLC available to purchase separately.  Stop making your returning/loyal/upgrading players pay for content they've already purchased.  I've been trying to buy a more-or-less "complete" version of Hitman 3/WoA for years, and I hear amazing things about Freelancer mode, but I couldn't give a !@#$ after this buying experience and seeing what your other customers have to say.  I'm sorry to the talented artists and programmers who clearly made a great game sadly buried in overly monetized anti-consumer business practices, making for one of the worst buying experiences in the industry, up there with EA, Ubisoft, and ActiBlizzKing.

title: HITMAN World of Assassination
Review Text: Finally, ♥♥♥♥ Epic store exclusivity. I get that Valve takes an unreasonably large cut from Steam sales. If IOI had sold this game direct or via itch.io, I would have bought it that way just to support them, but the Epic store is buggy, barely functional, anti-consumer trash even all these years after they launched it. title: HITMAN World of Assassination
Review Text: I know that was a lot of negative, but I do think they can fix a lot of these issues over time, and I genuinely love the Hitman trilogy. And even with just the standard edition, it is a LOT of content. I'm doing what I can to overcome the control hurdles, because the controls really do not translate well to VR. But I am having some fun with it. It's quite fun to screw with random NPC's in VR, and the amount of detail in each level is just amazing. Now that I'm seeing it in VR, I can really notice a lot more of the levels, and it is impressive. Long story short, if you're buying this to play it in VR, just don't. Save your money and wait until they fix these glaring issues, if they ever do. I'm going to keep it because I do have faith that the studio will fix it, but it is absurd that they released it like this.

title: Dispatch

title: Dispatch
Review Text: As for the plot, man what a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. The main villain is simply not there for 2/3 of the game. All of our decisions are extremely forced and non-consequential, and frankly, some choices just end up in scenes that objectively make more sense. The gameplay and story are totally separate, I mean, in the early game, my most sent hero was on the chopping block to be kicked out because of poor performance? There are also a few plot holes and unexplained events, but I don't want to spoil the plot, if you'd play the game you'd know how nonsensical the world is at times. My biggest annoyance, we seem to operate as a private company. I cannot imagine a world where such individuals are not heavily regulated by the government, and we just don't get any information about that. I truly wish the studio behind this game, rather than pay a lot for celebrity voice actors, paid someone to write a better plot and work on the world-building.

title: Dispatch

title: Dispatch
Review Text: Glad to see Telltale get a second life, but the Critical Role partnership should have been a red flag. The style and cute concept that sell the game are constantly fighting against aggressively cringeworthy dialogue where every character is transparently the same pervert Deadpool Reddit atheist in different wigs. There is so much precedent for "superheroes in the real world" as a subgenre, but somehow this manages to be the clumsiest of them all. It makes Incredibles look like I, Claudius. When AdHoc gets to do its thing and get you involved, it feels like a breath of fresh air from everything else.

title: Dispatch
Review Text: TLDR: Originally I rated this game a 4/10, but after my second playthrough I would give it a 6/10. Some really fun moments, fun gameplay, cool cast of characters, music was great and really cool fight scenes/animations. The voice acting was also insanely good! Unfortunately what REALLY killed the game (and leads me to leaving a negative review even though I had fun) was the horrible writing choices when it came to some dialogue/characters, sexual harassment/assault, outdated/immature "jokes/humour" and how there was a clear push for us to romance one character and the insane impact that one thing had on the overall story. It did have moments where it felt like our choices had some impact but not really when it came to the very last moment in the finale if you got invisigal's bad ending, what would happen to the villain wasn't even left to the player to make even though it was the entire point of the protag's personal story. Kinda a disappointment and felt like a waste of potential. Not sure if I would recommend this game or not, it has some cool moments but also some painful ones that leave you wishing for better writing.

title: Dispatch

Review Text: Playing solo, I load up the school map for the first time. I stop and appreciate how big and unnerving the map is. I check my equipment. I look around some more. I realize that I'm putting off going any further. The phone down the hall rings. After changing my pants, I start to hunt down where the ringing came from. All the way down the hall, huh? But the front door is all the way over here, and... oh, fine. I track down the room, pull out the EMF meter, which immediately jumps to 5. I throw down my book, ask the ghost to write in it (well, what came out was "Okaydothethinghurryup"), then sort of moonwalk my way back down the hall while chanting variations of "Nope". One Alt+F4 later, and I'm here writing this review. Nothing even happened, I simply psyched myself out. Now that's a horror game.

title: Phasmophobia
Review Text: Phas, it breaks my heart to leave a negative review. I've played your game for over 1770 hours. I've watched you push repetitive event content and ignore bugs for years. I've heard your promises of roadmap updates heard your promises of bug fixes, seen your lack of transparency (one holiday event was never even announced on steam till it was halfway over) and heard the stories of toxicity. When the player customization update got pushed and I saw just how big of a step backward it was, I told myself that surely this couldn't have been what we had been waiting for three years for you to deliver. The collab with Alan Wake? is a insult to Alan Wake. I hope that you take some time to seriously look at your game and your community and see how broken it is, how unhappy we are. But, if I'm being honest, I just don't think you will. The empty promises have eroded my trust to the point where I have none left. Hopefully you wake up before more people like me have moved on. I wanted SO much for you to succeed. I still do. Please be better. Do better. Fix the bugs. Focus on finishing your game before you collaborate with other people. Be more transparent. actually LISTEN to the feedback and community full of people who despite everything still want you to succeed.

title: Conan Exiles Enhanced
Review Text: Conan Exiles is on my favorite games list with Skyrim, the Dark Souls trilogy, and Minecraft. This game is truly special and a real gem. There is so much you can do in this game and it has long-term playability which makes it worth the money. You can play the story line or you can decide to do whatever you want instead. You have full access to the map unlike many other games. You can build all over the map in creative or in survival. It has crafting of all kinds of things. You can fight pve, or pvp if that is your thing. You can have your own private server. I just wish they allowed many worlds like in minecraft. You only get 2. Plus they allow mods and those add so much to the game. Some of the modders are truly gifted. I just wish they would keep their mods all up-to-date. You can play single player, co-op, or public. You can play online or offline. You have choices and I like that. But I have to say the most unique and best feature of Conan Exiles are the thralls. If you like to roleplay, then you will love the thralls. You can make the perfect companion to go on adventures with you, protect you or fight with you, and keep things operating at your base. I absolutely love the thralls. Best idea ever. This game never gets boring. Yes, it has some glitches and bugs (what game doesn't) but it doesn't detract from this being one of the best games ever made.

title: Conan Exiles Enhanced
Review Text: I would surmise that the best way to appreciate this game in a PVP setting would be on a limited and well modded server of maybe twenty to thirty people or so, with smaller clan size limits of perhaps five people per clan. Obviously the more like minded the players are on the server, the more enjoyable the experience will be for everyone. Alas, not everyone has ten or twenty friends they can get to play the same game, but this is one game which would truly shine above most given such a scenario. We definitely look forward to the server issues being improved, and we remain encouraged by the obvious care and time the devs are continuously putting into the game's development. Until then we will continue to enjoy this great experience and look forward for more to come as the game progresses.

Review Text: Amendment since people dont get it: Its not just about 'paying for a game you enjoy, you should be paying 100$ for every hour you spend playing.' its that the pricing has gone completely out of wack, they split up item sets and make it all time gated. The battle pass system is a 1 time purchase so you know what I can get over that since it pays you back the full amount. The bazaar is still just terrible, the blood crystal weapon set is split up between the battle pass, twitch drops and the bazaar so you cant get it all without paying ALOT, they are charging stupid pricing like 15$ for a table set of decor, the stormglass set already requires you to buy the isle of siptah but they put beautiful pieces for that set into the bazaar meaning if you want to make the best build with that set possible you need to fork over another $10, the latest Turian tent set isnt even complete with it missing vital pieces to even make a build with just those items and lastly everything is time gated. It is everything wrong with the system, overpriced, set splitting, incomplete and time gated. If these items were bundled up into DLC and sold as DLC packs I wouldnt have made this review. As I said I love the game, I love the old DLC model but im not going to support this cash grab. This review is the literal only way I, a random person online, can have any impact to it. title: Marathon
Review Text: As a Tarkov veteran (5k hours), I was intrigued by the game and its unique Art Direction, but I was a bit hesitant since the closed beta didn’t give the same vibe I saw in the cinematics and trailers. I gave it a shot during the Slam Test and now wow, I'm just as addicted as when I first played Tarkov. It finally feels like a really good extraction shooter and they really understood and respected the genre but added their own sauce. Even if you're not really attracted to the game, maybe for many reasons or because you think it lacks something, just give it a shot, you won’t regret it.

title: Marathon

title: Marathon
Review Text: If you don't have a mic and are worried that might hinder you, it won't really. From my experience with Hunt: Showdown I rarely use my mic (except to goad enemy teams in proxy chat) and this game has the beloved double-click Danger! Hunter! ping with a familiar loud noise other Hunt: Showdown players will be familiar with. So go ahead and play! A danger ping is all you need, trust me.

title: Marathon
Review Text: and, like, im a middling player, i can click heads fine but i panic in prolonged fire fights. really loving the thief for solo play, great shell for allowing me to be reactive and proactive in engagements and looting, lets me better go about exploring and taking in the world. theres frustrating performance issues on the latter two maps currently available, the cpu bound performance is putting my midrange computer through its paces as i maintain about 50fps under stress, but the grandeur and spectacle of outpost feels worth it. was so happy with the modest install size for a 2026 release, especially in this economy ;-;  it is a little crash prone, i fear, ive been hit with four crashes in 30 hours (40 something with the server slam). really hoping to see this live long enough for some of this to be worked out, i can somewhat understand the mixed emotions around bungie as stewards for this world and for the games around their unassailable gunplay, but i'm reviewing this kind of topically because i do believe in this in whatever way that entails.

title: Marathon
Review Text: Got to give credit where credit is due.  I was opposed to this game as I dont like games that are this competitive. But then I read this reddit post about spending more on a pizza and I thought, I am gamer who plays games. Why do I worry so much about these things. Just give it a try and if it sucks I am out one night of dinner out. And damn this game is really good. Creation wise.  The developers really did a nice job here. Super cool unique game that is hard AF . But I went in thinking you know what I am gonna die a lot probably. And after getting over that hump, I was able to just enjoy this game for what it is. A brutal horror shooter where everyone and everything wants to kill you. But the game itself is amazing. Yeah it might take me forever to progress but I dont care its just fun running around trying to get better learn the maps and avoid those  UESC  elite guys.  I think everyone needs to settle down with all this political stuff about games and just enjoy the games. Stop looking to argue over whether people should like it or not. Dont bother telling me about the player count or what sony and bungie are gonna do. I just want to share for someone that might be like me sitting there debating whether or not to play it. As its a game and we play games.

title: Marathon
Review Text: This game is great because of the weapons, lore, and art style. However, you really need a dedicated team to enjoy it. Playing with random players is too risky to justify using your best gear. While playing solo is fun for stealth, you’ll miss out on major parts of the game, like playing Cryo Archive, cleaning out the Pinwheel, or farming enough resources for high-level keys. Without a crew, the grind starts to feel pointless once you hit a certain level. If you have friends to play with, I highly recommend it. If you’re a solo player, you’ll likely get bored fast once you realize how much of the game is out of reach.

title: SnowRunner
Review Text: Absolutely awesome game. Very addictive, very tough. If you want a hardcore offroad simulator this is it, and its massive. After 80 hours im only 46% through the very first location, out of about 8 locations. The physics are great, the trucks and environment are very detailed and run very well buttery smooth at 60fps without any bugs at all, well, although some have reported things like floating trees, i have never encountered such glitches. Compared to Spintires and Mudrunner, this absolutely blows them away. The DLC is a bit on the pricey side for standalone trucks, but the maps are works of art and huge, and tbh are fairly priced especially if you get it all in a sale somewhere. Very high recommendation from me.

title: No Man's Sky
Review Text: Ok. Is someone actually still reading these reviews? Well if you get to this one, among the thousands of reviews for NMS, then you really are committed. As I'm sure you know by now, this game was an utter fail when it was released. The description of the game sounded so amazing, like everything you could ever want from a space-themed, sandbox builder. It was lame and repetitive with a low variety of content. The launch was done all wrong and, the term 'false advertising' was kicked around a lot. Well, luckily the developers didn't stop. It is now a complete game. There are SO many aspects to this game from flight (space or on planet), farming (mining), exploration, discovery, fighting off attacks from animals and drones (sentinels), to my favorite, amazing base-building with SO FREAKING MANY build options. They keep adding new ones too. Hello Games also recently released VR support, and it's really well done. There's something about being able to walk around in the base that you built, in real scale with VR. Hundreds of hours have gone into some of the most amazing bases I've built, and am planning for hundreds more.

title: No Man's Sky
Review Text: This game was like the ending of the TV Show "Lost", lots of buildup for a complete let down. Accumulate for no reason, talk for no reason, explore for no reason. I like a grind, but you don't even have anything to show for it. You'll never go back to wherever you were, and you'll never be able to share what you find with another human being. Cool, on this planet I'm going to name everything after memes. cool. What's the point? I'll never see that planet again when i leave for the next one. I gave this game far too much of a chance, and too much of my time. Go play minecraft, at least here you can build something. Play an MMO, at least there you can talk to someone. Go play a game with any form of campaign, at least you'll get a story. NMS is like all those games, without the things that make them great. No story, no meaningful building, no social. This feels entirely like an MMO that is empty. Accumulate wealth to buy nothing of worth, to quest with no one, and find nothing of note.

title: No Man's Sky
Review Text: Love it or hate it, this game is unfinished. The devs know it. Sony knows it. Everyone who bought this game knows it. It's able to sell because the game at heart has a great idea, and the devs and publisher know how to market it. The question is if you would pay $60 for an idea or a game. If you buy No Man's Sky, all you'll get is the former. If you're looking for the game, you won't find it here. They will tell you that you're buying the game (just look at these trailers and screenshots) but it's not. It's deceptive, disgusting, and borderline fraud, but it's business. Only purchase if you have faith in Hello Games, even after they lied to you.

title: No Man's Sky
Review Text: The fact that this title is still $60 says a lot about the ethics of Hello Games. The blatant lies told by the devs and the use of pre-rendered footage passed of as "actual gameplay" prior to release. The continuation of lies after the game was released. Yeah, they're adding new content (small amounts of it), but too little too late. It was a cleverly disguised sham of a game that took you around 4 hours to dig below the thin surface to find out what you thought you bought was nowhere near what you received, allowing both Hello Games and Steam to skirt the refund policy. Quite possibly one of the worst game puchases I've ever made in 30 years of gaming.

Review Text: 4) For a game that is all about flying around planets and landing on them - why in the heck can't I easily look DOWN? Didn't any of these spaceship manufactures ever get a request for a landing camera? Moms have these on their minivans so they don't back into the garage door FFS. Find something interesting fly near it and hit the LAND button and hope for the best. You might land where you want or you might fall into a canyon and be stuck. title: No Man's Sky
Review Text: I’ve really given this game a chance, but is not what it seems. Despite being presumably gigantic, is actually pretty poor in content. All resources look the same way on each planet, all the structures found on planets look the same way (I’ve found the same 3 types of aliens all across the explored universe). Even if somehow the planets are different (with different plants and ridiculous looking animals), you can feel that basically they are the same stuff. Also I’ve found that many of the animals start to look the same way after you’ve explored a number of planets. Also the space stations look EXACTLY the same in all solar systems. So bottom line, this game had been better off if the developers hand-made 10 awesome planets, instead of randomly generating endlessly the same things. Oh, and yes, there is not much to do in this game really and I’ve discovered over and over again the same technology (technology tree is very limited too).

title: Beholder: Conductor
Review Text: P.S. To be honest, I'm not quite sure where the franchise should go from here. The success of the first game led to Alawar making a franchise out of it. However, as we've seen after 3 attempts, they just can't pull the proverbial recapture of lightning in the bottle. If I had to oversimplify it by singling out a reason though, I'd say the promotion-centric gameplay is at the root of the issue, because it breaks one of Beholder's core pillars in that your choices matter. In the ideal world, you should be able to choose not to get promoted - and the game should respect your choice and provide you with more quests and characters. This content, however, would be invisible to other players and would likely push the scope and budget of such game outside of what Alawar can reasonably allocate to a Beholder title. And do we even need another Beholder title? The themes Beholder games explore felt quaint and novel a decade ago, but this days? This days authoritarian regimes are on the rise and freedom of speech is challenged in many countries across the globe, to the point what used to feel like fantasy now feels like a documentary - a sad reminder of the world outside the window. We play games for escapism, not to be sad, right?

Review Text: I have absolutely no issues with the gameplay, character/monster design, combat or anything like that. Monster Hunter games in my eyes are meant to be played together with friends and I doubt that this would be an unpopular opinion. The developers decided to make the main story missions of this game a terrible multiplayer experience. All players must individually seek out the monster, watch a cutscene, quit the mission and only then can you invite your friends into the lobby, making you run to the monster all over again. This is so absurd it sounds like a joke, how can this be real, how can these devs be so incompetent? Even after thousands upon thousands of complaints from players and years passing Capcom just decides to plug their ears and not listen. Ridiculous.

title: SILENT HILL 2
Review Text: The heaviest burden isn’t just losing someone you love. It’s knowing your selfish choices led to their end. Every step, every thought reminds you of it and you wonder if you’ll ever forgive yourself for what’s been done. Silent Hill 2 constantly makes you feel the consequences of your own actions, turning every choice and every misstep into a haunting reminder. The game doesn’t rely on cheap jumpscares to frighten you. With its terrifying creatures, dark, claustrophobic environments, unsettling atmosphere, haunting music and occasional whispers, cries, and voices from the unseen, it delivers a truly unique experience. One that proves it’s a masterpiece of its genre.

title: Burglin' Gnomes
Review Text: This game is really fun in a lot of ways but the enemy scaling is insane. If the big guy dies then dozens of little enemies show up to repeatedly kill you, they are faster than you, climb up everything with ease, and hit you and knock you out over and over without a break for you to get up and run. You can never hit them fast enough since your character swings weird and slow so defending yourself just isn't an option and it wouldn't matter anyway cause they all instantly respawn. I cannot stress enough how impossible it is to play with genuinely like 15 faster, stronger, up your butt enemies that have the sole goal to make sure you fail. You can obviously have the idea to not kill the man yourself but sometimes he does it to himself with a grenade or missile launcher or whatever else and it isnt as rare that he kills himself as you'd hope. It's also worth mentioning that it doesn't just take the old guy dying to cause them to spawn, sometimes they just do. The upstairs is really nice but it's buggy and easy to glitch or not move sometimes up there or on the stairs. Outside of all this, the game is really fun, the premise of the game is wonderful, but the enemies make the game unplayable and unenjoyable to an extreme. I would not recommend the game as it stands with this level of difficulty, I would wait to see that something is done about the enemies to make them more manageable unless you feel like restarting the game over and over with nothing.

title: The Planet Crafter
Review Text: I've been playing this game since very early access. I have watched how its grown and improved over time and how they listened to what they're community was saying and requesting. I remember sending a request report asking for co-op, a few months later they made it work. I got my friends to start playing with me and our only real complaint was the time it took to get from one end of the map to the other. They listened to the community and added teleporters and a vehicle. The community wanted a bit more endgame, they added the portal generator and the free DLC moons that you can go too after beating your planet. They've just released a 2nd paid DLC for even more planets to explore and they made the non-greedy decision to make it so only host has to have DLC. This game and community is loved and cared for by the developers and I'm beyond proud to recommend this game to anyone and everyone.

If anything, the "you can only build in X order" only gets worse in the mid to late game (I progressed well into the insect stage and the gaps between new build items gets wider and wider). That said, you know the bit where I say the blueprints you find in boxes are non-required QOL items? NOPE! One of them is critical to advance the story of the game... and it's FAARRRRR down the "optional" blueprint order. title: The Planet Crafter
Review Text: A lot of reviews seem to be comparing this to Subnautica. Nah. First of all, there's no friction here. In Subnautica, one was forced to go further out beyond the reef and deeper into the abyss to find crafting materials and blueprints, which meant facing your fears, girding your loins, having your wits about you. In this game, not only do the blueprints come to you via your research module, the materials come to you too, via meteor storms (with incredibly weak-sauce impacts). Even when you do explore, there's so little to see; this game lacks all the visual and stylistic richness of Subnautica. The sound design is absolutely dead. The things you're collecting are just chunks of varying shades of grey. Nothing feels like anything.

title: The Planet Crafter
Review Text: This is a very relaxing game to play, especially if you are stoned or had a few beers. It's so simple to play and there really is no threat to the player except for the first 30 minutes or so. But this game does not even hold a candle to Subnautica, not even close. Resource gathering is essential to the game but it feels so cheap because you are not breaking down rocks or digging to get to the materials but just picking them up off the ground. The material recipes for many things don't make any sense. Want to build a nuclear reactor? Well that will be 2 water bottles and a uranium rod. There is also a large unsightly seam in the map where you can see through the ground. The map is small. Exploration is boring, there is not much to see or do. There are so many better alternatives to this game at the moment. But i'm interested to see where it goes!

title: Captain of Industry
Review Text: Gaaaah!  Another addictive game in the vein of Factorio and Dyson Sphere.  Concise description: Contemporary Industrial Automation.  Large tech tree.  I'm "only" 60 hours in and haven't finished a whole game yet, so I will probably comment further later.  This isn't science fiction with magic robots and drones (yet) or production and supply chain you can't relate to; this is how it's done in real life (with some simplification).  You think you can just suck oil out of the ground and be on you way?  Boy, you gots to distill and crack them hydrocarbons dontcha know.  Don't tell your kids, but it will probably make them learn stuff. I learned a lot about the petrochemical industry,  metal production from iron, steel and copper.  And farming too.   Even made me research them out of curiosity.  I'm a retired engineer, so it got my Geek going on. This is what powers civilization.  It's still under development but very playable now, I havent hit any game destroying bugs.  Bend nature to your will, but don't piss away resources.  This will keep you playing late into the night.

title: Captain of Industry
Review Text: I've played the big 3 automation games and can safely say that the 4th horseman of the automation apocalypse is right here. There's a lot of B and C tier automation games popping up. title: Captain of Industry
Review Text: Overall, I think think is a very solid start and a lot of fun, with tons of content for an early access game. There are definitely some other balance and progression things that need work in addition to what I mentioned above, and I plea with the devs to focus on vehicle pathing before anything else. It's by far the most frustrating part of the game, but otherwise, I'm really excited to see how this game continues to progress and I'm having a lot of fun!

Review Text: I've played a lot of production simulator games (Factorio, Satisfactory, car production simulators, etc.) this is one of the best. I worked in an industrial production environment as an engineer my whole career (nuclear power, oil refining, and polymer production), and this game comes as close as I've seen to being realistic as far as production chains go (raws in, finished products out)... not perfect, but the inaccuracies are probably to maintain playability.  A definite buy!

Review Text: Unfortunately, the devs have fumbled it from a management perspective.  Paid DLC during EA is a bad look, but not the end of the world. Review Text: UPDATE: So it's now known that for next year, there won't be a standalone F1 26 game, but rather a DLC expansion that will include the 2026 season, with a new game being released in 2027. Now, this is actually nothing new that Codemasters and EA have done, because last year in 2024, they released a 2024 Season Expansion for EA Sports WRC. That one did include the drivers, cars and tracks of that year, including ORLEN Rally Poland and Tet Rally Latvia. Even though only one new circuit for 2026 is confirmed, the Madring in Madrid, Spain. It's unknown of it's gonna be added to the game. People seem to be a bit interested in it since 2026 will have the new regulations take effect. And the last times Codemasters made a game on the year when new regs hit (F1 2014 and F1 22) were considered flops, even though I didn't really have any problems with them. This new season expansion and new game in 2027 confirms that Codemasters and EA are still gonna be making the F1 games since people were speculating that Codemasters and EA would not be making F1 games since their deal was set to expire at the end of the year.

title: Valheim
Review Text: I'm an older player. (in my seventies), but it was my sons who introduce me to the game. Though them have since moved on I continue to use Valheim on a regular basis.  I mostly use the game for two things. Keeping my memory active, and for building things. I've built lots and lots of shelters of various kinds, and am continuing to improve  the quality and techniques. To do so I pretty much operate with the development commands to make things easier for doing what  I like best. I continue to learn how to protect the shelters and myself from the various enemies. I like the graphics which have improved since I first started. For real gamers, it offers some great challenges.

title: Valheim
Review Text: i'm a 63 year old gamer.  Been playing since 1977 when the Atari 2600 came out with "Pong" as the big thing. I have primarily enjoyed open-world exploration games, like Oblivion, Skyrim, and Fallout4, which I have played many hours on.   I have already done 555 hours on Valheim, which has some the most amazingly beautiful, realistic, not to mention dangerous biomes I have ever seen, despite the low detail.  The different climate effects mixed with the lightling and weather are superb,  And the sound effects are so realistic, especially the lightning and thunder storms.  The crafting and settlement building along with you picking where you're going to settle is so great! Definitely one of the best games I have ever played!

title: Valheim
Review Text: In my case at the very least, I had the foresight to forget about this game for these past 4 years and get a little frustrated at the occasional previously mentioned non-update posts so I can come back when the Deep North releases and have more than a single thing to do in the game.  I cant remember if I saw the Mistlands yet, I might have during a one-off adventure day with a friend but, at this point I'll want to start from scratch when I play again.  So having 2 or 3 new biomes to see will be nice.  But this game is great, it set new standards and innovated in some places over other games, however small those bits may be.  There are things I can do in Valheim that feel like a drag in other games, like the building system.  But ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, if only the game could take all the potential it has and actually do SOMETHING with it besides making me wait for them to update the game once every blood moon...

title: Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas – The Definitive Edition
Review Text: San Andreas Definitive Edition is a decent effort, but unfortunately, it inherits many of the same issues that plagued its predecessors. While it's a shame that Rockstar didn't iron out these wrinkles, my bigger gripe is that they removed the classic versions from Steam. Those originals, flaws and all, still hold a certain charm and would've provided a welcome alternative for players seeking a more authentic experience. As it stands, this Definitive Edition is a middling attempt at reviving a beloved game, leaving me wondering what could've been if Rockstar had simply given us the option to play the originals.

title: Hunt: Showdown 1896
Review Text: Read other people's reviews to get an idea of how good this game is mechanically. What I rarely see discussed is how well  Hunt: Showdown works as a social experience. Because you are playing with only one or two players at once and there is a fair amount of down time between fights, you spend a lot of time just chatting. It's easy to make new friends in random queue, most of my steam friends list comes from this game. As you play you'll gain characters and money, but you'll also lose those things in the blink of an eye. Friends last longer and they are more valuable.
